title: Quantum phase diagrams for bosons in hexagonal optical potentials: A continuous-space quantum Monte Carlo study
abstract: Hexagonal optical lattices, emulating graphene and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) structures, provide a versatile platform for exploring strongly correlated quantum matter. Using continuous-space exact diagonalization and quantum Monte Carlo simulations, we investigate the phase diagrams of ultracold bosons in honeycomb and h-BN lattices. For the honeycomb lattice, we find significant deviations from the standard Bose-Hubbard model even for strong lattice amplitudes. We observe suppressed Mott insulator lobes and the absence of higher-order insulating phases, attributed to strong density-assisted tunneling effects. In the h-BN case, a rich phase diagram emerges, featuring multiple Mott lobes with various sublattice occupations, driven by the interplay of lattice asymmetry, interactions, and particle filling. Our results highlight the necessity of continuous-space treatments for capturing the full complexity of bosonic quantum phases in hexagonal geometries, paving the way for experimental realizations with ultracold atoms and further theoretical work.
summary: This paper uses continuous-space quantum Monte Carlo to study bosons in honeycomb and h-BN-like optical lattices. It demonstrates that the standard Bose-Hubbard model fails at higher fillings due to density-assisted tunneling and reveals complex phase diagrams driven by lattice asymmetry. These findings are crucial for the accurate modeling of ultracold atoms in hexagonal geometries.
main_problem: Investigating the quantum phase diagrams of ultracold bosons in hexagonal and h-BN-like optical potentials and determining the validity limits of the standard Bose-Hubbard model.
main_result: The study finds that density-assisted tunneling significantly suppresses higher-order Mott insulator lobes, and a rich phase diagram with various sublattice occupations emerges in asymmetric h-BN-like lattices.
method: Continuous-space exact diagonalization for single-particle band structures and worm-algorithm quantum path-integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) for many-body phase diagrams.
model_or_system: A 2D gas of identical spinless bosons with repulsive contact interactions in hexagonal (honeycomb) and asymmetric (h-BN) optical lattices, modeled via a continuous-space Hamiltonian and an extended Bose-Hubbard model.
key_observables: Local particle density, sublattice occupations (nA, nB), compressibility, superfluid fraction, and superfluid phase-space density.
important_parameters: Lattice depth (V0), geometric phase (phi_g), interaction strength (g), chemical potential (mu), and tunneling amplitudes (J, J', J_tilde).
main_assumptions: The interaction is modeled as a contact (s-wave) interaction, and the evaluation of density-assisted tunneling uses a mean-field approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows lattice geometry and vectors; Figure 2 displays single-particle band dispersions; Figure 4 presents PIMC results for density and superfluidity; Figure 7 illustrates the evolution of the lattice potential with geometric phase; Figure 8 shows the relative correction from density-assisted tunneling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the hexagonal lattice geometry, presents single-particle band structure calculations via exact diagonalization, details the many-body PIMC simulations, analyzes the phase diagrams for symmetric and asymmetric cases, quantifies deviations from the Bose-Hubbard model, and concludes with the impact of density-assisted tunneling.
why_interesting: It provides critical insights into how continuous-space effects like density-assisted tunneling break the standard tight-binding paradigm in hexagonal geometries, which is essential for designing accurate experiments with ultracold atoms in graphene-like lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Hexagonal lattice. (a) Lattice vectors Gj entering the potential of Eq. (2). Faint arrows indicate the laser wave vectors in the implementation of Ref. [36]. (b) Lattice formed by the minima of the potential in Eq. (2). It is composed of two interpenetrating triangular sublattices A (red) and B (green). Also shown are the Bravais vectors a1,2, the nearest- neighbor vectors δj, and the next-nearest-neighbor vectors δ′ j.

Figure 2. Single-particle band structure for a honeycomb potential with various amplitudes and geometric phases. Results obtained using exact diagonalization of Hamiltonian ˆH0. (a) Full dispersion relations in the first Brillouin zone for the first bands, V0 = 15 Er, and ϕg = 0. (b1) Band dispersion relations along the high-symmetry path Γ–K1–M–K2 for V0 = 5 Er, and (b1) ϕg = 0, (b2) ϕg = 0.008, and (b3) ϕg = 0.1. (c) Same as (b) but for V0 = 15 Er.

Figure 3. Tight-binding parameters as functions of the geometric phase ϕg for V0 = 15 Er. Results obtained by fitting the lowest-band dispersion relation of the continuous single-particle Hamiltonian ˆH0 to the nn (blue), nnn (red), and triangular (black) models. The vertical gray line marks ϕ∗ g ≃0.25, where the lowest B-site state crosses the first ex- cited A-site state. (a) Sublattice offset ∆AB obtained from the nn (blue) and nnn (red) model (note that they overlap each other), together with a linear fit (dashed black line). (b) Tun- neling amplitudes: J (solid), J′ A (dashed), and J′ B (dotted) for each model. (c) and (d) Normalized fitting errors for, respec- tively, the lower and...

Figure 4. Quantum Monte Carlo simulation results for 2D bosons in a honeycomb optical lattice (V0 = 15 Er, ϕg = 0, ˜g0 = 0.67) for several temperatures and system sizes. (a) Density n, compressibility κ, and superfluid fraction fs versus chemical potential for several temperatures (the ticks on the colorbar indicate the used temperatures). (b) Superfluid phase-space density nsλ2 T versus temperature for various system sizes L×L (indicated on te colorbar), for µ ≃2.17 Er. The black horizontal line indicates the expacted BKT superfluid transition.

Figure 5. Quantum phase diagram for bosons in the honey- comb lattice (ϕg = 0) versus chemical potential and interac- tion strength for (a) V0 = 15 Er and (b) V0 = 9.75 Er Results obtained using continuous-space QMC for kBT = 0.01 Er, Colored regions denote superfluid (light green), normal-fluid (dark green), and Mott insulator (purple-mauve) phases. The crosses indicate selected boundary points obtained numeri- cally with error bars corresponding to ˜g0–µ space discretiza- tion; only a subset is shown for visual clarity.
